title: 2 Chronicles 3
subtitle: The Temple Built on Mount Moriah

sections.header: Verses 1–9 — Foundations on Moriah; the Main Hall

heb.paragraph: Solomon builds the temple on Mount Moriah (v.1) — the only biblical text that identifies the temple site with the place where Abraham nearly sacrificed Isaac (Gen 22:2). The Chronicler connects three events at one location: Abraham’s binding of Isaac, David’s altar at the threshing floor, and Solomon’s temple. Sacrifice upon sacrifice upon sacrifice.

refs.note: “Go to the region of Moriah. Sacrifice him there.” Abraham’s near-sacrifice of Isaac. The temple rises where the substitute ram died.
The Kings parallel provides more architectural detail; Chronicles adds the Moriah connection.

notes.note: Building on Moriah teaches that the place of supreme sacrifice becomes the place of perpetual worship. Where Abraham offered Isaac, Solomon builds for God. Obedience creates sacred ground. Calvin's reading characteristically emphasizes divine sovereignty and human responsibility in tension — God ordains the outcome while the actors remain morally accountable. This dual emphasis reflects Calvin's broader hermeneutic: Scripture always speaks both about God's plan and about human duty under that plan.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: har hammōriyyāh is the only occurrence linking the temple mount to Gen 22:2. Jewish tradition universally accepted this identification, which became foundational for Jerusalem’s theology of sacred space. The NET translators note that the underlying Hebrew/Greek syntax carries nuances that most English versions cannot fully represent. Lexical analysis of the key terms reveals semantic ranges that enrich the passage beyond what a single English gloss can convey, connecting this text to a wider web of biblical usage.

hist.context: Construction begins in Solomon’s fourth year, on the site David purchased from Ornan. The Chronicler adds the Moriah identification absent from Kings — connecting the temple to Abraham’s supreme act of faith. Dimensions: 60 cubits long, 20 wide. The portico is 20 cubits wide and 120 cubits high (an enormous figure, perhaps symbolic). The interior is overlaid with gold, decorated with palm trees and chains, and set with precious stones.

notes.note: The Chronicler records this history not merely as political narrative but as theological instruction. Every event demonstrates God's sovereign governance of His people and His unwavering faithfulness to the Davidic covenant. The believing reader is meant to see in these events the pattern of God's dealings with His church in every age.

The Chronicler's perspective consistently emphasizes God's faithfulness to His covenant promises despite Israel's unfaithfulness. This dual theme — human failure and divine grace — is the heartbeat of all Scripture and finds its ultimate resolution in the cross of Christ, where God's justice and mercy meet perfectly (Rom 3:25-26).

The Chronicler wrote for a post-exilic community needing to understand their identity and hope. His message remains vital: God's promises to David are irrevocable, worship is the center of covenant life, and faithfulness — not circumstances — determines blessing. Believers today can take the same comfort: our God is faithful even when we are not (2 Tim 2:13).

sections.header: Verses 10–17 — The Most Holy Place; the Cherubim; the Pillars

refs.note: The Kings description of the cherubim. Chronicles adds that they face outward, toward the main hall.
The tabernacle veil. Solomon’s veil uses the same colours: blue, purple, and crimson.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: yākîn (“he establishes”) and bōʿaz (“in him is strength”) — the names function as theological declarations permanently inscribed at the temple entrance. The NET translators note that the underlying Hebrew/Greek syntax carries nuances that most English versions cannot fully represent. Lexical analysis of the key terms reveals semantic ranges that enrich the passage beyond what a single English gloss can convey, connecting this text to a wider web of biblical usage.

hist.context: The Most Holy Place is a perfect 20-cubit cube, overlaid with 600 talents of gold. Two cherubim of olive wood, each with 5-cubit wings, span the entire width of the room (20 cubits wing-tip to wing-tip). They stand facing outward — guarding the entrance to God’s presence. The veil of blue, purple, and crimson separates the holy from the most holy. Two bronze pillars, Jakin and Boaz, stand before the temple — the same pillars Nebuchadnezzar will break up in 2 Kings 25:13.

heb.paragraph: Bayit (house) carries a deliberate double meaning in Chronicles: Solomon builds God a house (temple), and God promises David a house (dynasty). The interplay between these two 'houses' generates the theological tension that drives the entire narrative: will the dynasty endure? Will the temple remain? Both depend on covenant faithfulness.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVHe paneled the main hall with juniper and covered it with fine gold and decorated it with palm tree and chain designs.ESVThe nave he lined with cypress and covered it with fine gold and made palms and chains on it.NIVThe portico at the front of the temple was twenty cubits long across the width of the building and twenty cubits high. He overlaid the inside with pure gold.ESVThe vestibule in front of the nave of the house was twenty cubits long, equal to the width of the house, and its height was 120 cubits. He overlaid it on the inside with pure gold.

themes.note: The Temple Built on Mount Moriah

coaching.tip: The Chronicler specifies that Solomon builds the temple on Mount Moriah — 'where the LORD had appeared to his father David... on the threshing floor of Araunah.' The site connects three moments: Abraham's near-sacrifice of Isaac, David's altar during plague, and Solomon's temple. Sacred geography accumulates meaning.

prayer_prompt: The temple was built on Mount Moriah — where Abraham almost sacrificed Isaac. Consider asking God about the sacred sites in your life where sacrifice and provision intersect.
